evidence_inventory.title: Small-Town Campus Transportation & Coordination Gaps

core_papers.title: Examining network governance of multimodal integration: A comparative study of rural mobility hubs in The Netherlands
core_papers.role: background motivator
core_papers.key_finding: Shows that rural mobility access often depends on coordination across multiple actors and modes; it supports a coordination-heavy setting rather than a campus-specific rideshare sh
core_papers.citation_note: Use to say the mobility coordination problem is documented in rural integration research.

core_papers.title: How to ensure sufficient access to public transport in rural areas? A comparative analysis of institutional designs for multimodal integration
core_papers.role: background motivator
core_papers.key_finding: Compares institutional designs for rural public transport access and indicates that service sufficiency is shaped by governance and integration choices.
core_papers.citation_note: Supports the narrower claim that small-town and rural access is often organizationally complex.

core_papers.title: Analyzing the impact of demand management in rural shared mobility-on-demand systems
core_papers.role: background motivator
core_papers.key_finding: Indicates that sparse-demand shared mobility in rural settings faces bundling and demand-management constraints.
core_papers.citation_note: Useful for operational fragility, not for student-specific unmet demand.

core_papers.title: An importance-performance analysis of public transport to the university campus based on best-worst scaling
core_papers.role: campus-adjacent contextualizer
core_papers.key_finding: Documents that campus-bound riders care about specific public transport attributes and that campus access quality can vary.
core_papers.citation_note: Supports campus transport salience, but not peer-rideshare scarcity.

core_papers.title: Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airbnb
core_papers.role: trust contextualizer
core_papers.key_finding: Shows that profile photos act as trust signals in stranger exchange settings.
core_papers.citation_note: Use to explain why peer coordination can face trust frictions.

core_papers.title: The Effects of Online Trust-Building Mechanisms on Trust in the Sharing Economy: The Perspective of Providers
core_papers.role: trust contextualizer
core_papers.key_finding: Finds that reviews, identity verification, and social-media-linked cues shape perceived trust in sharing platforms.
core_papers.citation_note: Supports the claim that participation frictions are partly trust-mediated.

evidence_inventory.narrative: A narrower rural and small-town transport coordination problem is documented in the A-category mobility literature, while peer-platform trust friction is documented in the B-category trust literature. Together this motivates a small-town campus transport coordination question and points to a plausible reason students might turn to peer-organized rides. The corpus does not directly establish that small-town university students are underserved by commercial ridesharing, so Beat 1 should stay at the level of contextual motivation.
remaining_gaps: Direct campus-specific study of rideshare scarcity in rural or small-town universities is missing.
No paper directly shows that campus populations compensate through peer ride coordination.
Campus evidence is transit-quality oriented, not peer-rideshare-gap oriented.
Motivation remains contextual rather than direct evidence for Beats 4-5.

The corpus can motivate a narrower claim that rural/small-town mobility systems are coordination-heavy and often imperfect, with general peer-platform trust frictions layered on top. It does not directly establish that small-town university students are underserved by commercial ridesharing or that campus populations specifically compensate through peer coordination.

evidence_inventory.title: Grassroots Coordination and Integrated Campus Platforms

core_papers.title: Bridging the Informal and Formal Learning Spaces with WhatsApp
core_papers.role: Establishes the campus-adjacent premise that WhatsApp can bridge formal and informal university life, so messaging groups can be treated as existing coordination infrastructure rather than an unfamiliar behavior.
core_papers.key_finding: WhatsApp has potential to bridge formal and informal learning spaces for university faculty and students in Namibia and India.

core_papers.title: How Social Media as a Digital Marketing Strategy Influences Chinese Students' Decision to Study Abroad in the United States: A Model Analysis Approach
core_papers.role: Provides the international-student layer by showing that social media significantly shapes Chinese students' expectations and decisions around study abroad, indicating that platform-mediated coordination is already embedded in mobility experiences.
core_papers.key_finding: Social media as a digital marketing tool significantly shapes Chinese students' perceptions, expectations, and decision-making processes regarding studying in the United States.

core_papers.title: Cooperative affordances: How instant messaging apps afford learning, resistance and solidarity among food delivery workers
core_papers.role: Shows that people routinely appropriate private instant-messaging groups as parallel coordination infrastructure when official platforms do not support mutual aid, learning, or solidarity well enough.
core_papers.key_finding: Platform workers appropriate private instant messaging chat groups to restore mutualism, build communities of practice, and organize resistance not afforded by official delivery ap

core_papers.title: Residents' WeChat Group Use and Pro-Community Behavior in the COVID-19 Crisis: A Distal Mediating Role of Community Trust and Community Attachment.
core_papers.role: Extends the coordination argument beyond one task by indicating that WeChat groups can build trust, attachment, and pro-community behavior, supporting the idea of one social channel carrying rides, help-seeking, exchange, and other local coordination.
core_papers.key_finding: Residents' WeChat group use positively impacts pro-community behavior through the mediating roles of community trust and community attachment during COVID-19.

core_papers.title: Local super apps in the 15-minute city: a new model for sustainable smart cities?
core_papers.role: Introduces the super-app line as an initial inquiry: bundling mobility and neighborhood services into a local app may reduce fragmentation, but the framing is city-scale and only partially transferable to campuses.
core_papers.key_finding: Integrating Mobility-as-a-Feature with the 15-minute city concept into local super apps can drive equitable and sustainable urban transitions with seamless service access.

evidence_inventory.narrative: C-category papers document that messaging apps can enable community coordination in adjacent settings. D-category papers document that
remaining_gaps: This beat is only partially supported. C suggests messaging apps can enable community coordination in adjacent contexts, and D supports the broader importance of international-student support networks, but the leap to transport-specific self-organization is thin. I appears sparse, very recent, and weakly connected, so the move from grassroots chat groups to a multi-module campus platform remains more an informed inquiry than a well-established literature line.

evidence_inventory.title: Four Design Primitives: Identity, Safety, Rating Fairness, Rewards

core_papers.title: Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airbnb
core_papers.role: Identity/trust proxy for the institutional-identity primitive: a classic paper on how visible identity cues shape trust formation on platforms. We use it cautiously because direct campus-.edu identity literature is sparse in this candidate set.
core_papers.key_finding: Personal photos serve as trust signals on Airbnb, influencing reputation formation and booking decisions in the sharing economy.

core_papers.title: A Comprehensive Study on Mobile SOS and Personal Safety Applications Across Multiple Scenarios
core_papers.role: Representative safety-infrastructure paper: synthesizes SOS, live location, and sensor-assisted safety apps, which lets the section define safety as a distinct design primitive even though the literature here is recent and implementation-heavy.
core_papers.key_finding: Personal safety apps are shifting from manual panic-button models to context-aware, sensor-fused, AI-assisted systems integrated with police or commercial monitoring centers, yet s

core_papers.title: A Systematic Literature Review of Anti-Discrimination Design Strategies in the Digital Sharing Economy
core_papers.role: Framework anchor for rating fairness: shows that sharing-economy platforms embed design decisions that advantage some groups over others, supporting a neutral definition of rating fairness as its own primitive.
core_papers.key_finding: DSE platforms embed design decisions that allow certain demographics to gain unfair advantage, identifiable through systematic synthesis of 58 interdisciplinary studies.

core_papers.title: Gamification for climate change engagement: review of corpus and future agenda
core_papers.role: Representative rewards/gamification paper: positions gamification as a commonly discussed motivational layer that can shape engagement and sustainable behavior without implying it is universally necessary or superior.
core_papers.key_finding: Games and gamification show promising potential to impact multiple climate change engagement dimensions simultaneously.

core_papers.title: Creating a trusting environment in the sharing economy: Unpacking mechanisms for trust-building used by peer-to-peer carpooling platforms
core_papers.role: Recent carpooling-specific trust-building study; used as the nearest domain analogue for institutional identity, while noting that trust mechanisms vary by user experience and platform context.
core_papers.key_finding: Trust-building mechanisms in P2P carpooling platforms have differential effects depending on car ownership and user experience level.

evidence_inventory.narrative: Keep the tone definitional and parallel: 'we distill,' 'we propose,' 'commonly discussed.' Do not rank the four primitives in this beat. Be explicit that the institutional-identity strand is the weakest in the provided set: the best available evidence is adjacent identity/trust work, not direct .edu or campus-scope studies. Also note that the safety strand is mostly recent app/system literature, so it supports a practical design pattern (real-time location, SOS, safe routing) more than a long-established theoretical tradition. The fairness paragraph should emphasize that rating systems are socially and algorithmically shaped rather than neutral, but should not claim that any one mitigation fully solves bias. The rewards paragraph should present gamification as an optional motivational layer, not as a necessary ingredient.
remaining_gaps: The framework has partial support, but the four primitives are not equally grounded. Identity verification appears especially under-supported as a direct literature line, safety evidence is thin, and the fairness and gamification literatures do not integrate into a coherent shared framework in this corpus. This means the primitives work better as defensible design proposals than as fully literature-derived necessities.

evidence_inventory.title: Passenger-Side WTP and Motivations

core_papers.title: Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airbnb
core_papers.role: Contextualizing reference from category B: Trust and reputation in the sharing economy: The role of personal photos in Airb
core_papers.key_finding: Personal photos serve as trust signals on Airbnb, influencing reputation formation and booking decisions in the sharing economy.

core_papers.title: Algorithmic Management in Organizations? From Edge Case to Center Stage
core_papers.role: Contextualizing reference from category J: Algorithmic Management in Organizations? From Edge Case to Center Stage
core_papers.key_finding: Algorithmic management creates a gray zone blurring employee-freelancer distinctions, allowing platforms to circumvent traditional employment regulations.

core_papers.title: Fighting bias with bias: How same-race endorsements reduce racial discrimination on Airbnb
core_papers.role: Contextualizing reference from category H: Fighting bias with bias: How same-race endorsements reduce racial discrimination
core_papers.key_finding: White guests largely overcame racial bias in Airbnb host selection when hosts were endorsed by previous white guests, showing same-race endorsements offset rather than reinforce di

core_papers.title: Creating a trusting environment in the sharing economy: Unpacking mechanisms for trust-building used by peer-to-peer carpooling platforms
core_papers.role: Contextualizing reference from category B: Creating a trusting environment in the sharing economy: Unpacking mechanisms for
core_papers.key_finding: Trust-building mechanisms in P2P carpooling platforms have differential effects depending on car ownership and user experience level.

core_papers.title: A Systematic Literature Review of Anti-Discrimination Design Strategies in the Digital Sharing Economy
core_papers.role: Contextualizing reference from category H: A Systematic Literature Review of Anti-Discrimination Design Strategies in the D
core_papers.key_finding: DSE platforms embed design decisions that allow certain demographics to gain unfair advantage, identifiable through systematic synthesis of 58 interdisciplinary studies.

evidence_inventory.narrative: Beat 4 is §4.1 passenger-side survey findings (primary_data). The anchor is the pseudo-anchor local:CornellCarpoolSurvey2026 (N=117 formative survey). The spine references findings (F1/F3/F4) and section labels, not paperIds. Narrative: (1) recap N=117 / 50 complete / 82% Mandarin scope disclosure, (2) transport-gap quantification (F1), (3) WTP safety-feature ranking (F3: location 70.1%, .edu 65.9%, SOS 64.4%), (4) motivation structure (F4: financial 63.6%, gamification 48.3%, social 45.6%, environmental 44.5%). NO inferential statistics, NO p-values, NO 'significantly'. Use: we observe, reports indicate, median value is. Disclose 82% Mandarin-native as scope, not mask as bias. (Primary-data / artifact beat: chain assembled from manual_core_inclusions.primary_anchors; contextualizing literature is listed as supporting rather than spine.)
remaining_gaps: This beat is supported primarily by the paper's own formative survey, which is appropriate for a descriptive findings beat. The limitation is scope: the sample is skewed and 82% Mandarin-native, so the evidence should be framed as formative design input for a specific campus subpopulation rather than general student demand.

evidence_inventory.title: Driver-Side Tolerance and Rating-Fairness Asymmetry

core_papers.title: Fairness in Algorithmic Management: Bringing Platform-Workers into the Fold
core_papers.role: Provides the main interpretive frame: perceived fairness of managerial algorithms matters to workers, making it plausible that evaluative unfairness would be salient even in a smaller peer-driving context.
core_papers.key_finding: Independent platform workers can experience perceived organizational support, and the distributive fairness of managerial algorithms critically influences their job satisfaction.

core_papers.title: Trust and power in Airbnb’s digital rating and reputation system
core_papers.role: Moves from abstract fairness to the specific mechanics of digital ratings, arguing that reputation systems can embed power asymmetries rather than function as neutral trust signals.
core_papers.key_finding: Digital reputation systems on platforms like Airbnb embed power asymmetries that undermine their reliability as fair communicators of trustworthy market behavior.

core_papers.title: A Systematic Literature Review of Anti-Discrimination Design Strategies in the Digital Sharing Economy
core_papers.role: Broadens the frame from one platform to a synthesis of anti-discrimination design strategies, showing that unfair outcomes can be built into digital-sharing interfaces and policies.
core_papers.key_finding: DSE platforms embed design decisions that allow certain demographics to gain unfair advantage, identifiable through systematic synthesis of 58 interdisciplinary studies.

core_papers.title: Enhancing the Reputation System in  Ridesharing: A Blockchain-Based Mutual Escrow and Cool-Off Period Approach
core_papers.role: Supplies a design-response paper focused on retaliatory feedback and manipulation, offering concrete mechanisms such as mutual escrow and cool-off periods that speak directly to rating-fairness concerns.
core_papers.key_finding: Mutual escrow, bidirectional dynamic rating, and a cooldown period reduce rating manipulation and retaliatory feedback in decentralized ride-sharing platforms.

core_papers.title: A Comprehensive Study on Mobile SOS and Personal Safety Applications Across Multiple Scenarios
core_papers.role: Marks the scope boundary for Category F: adjacent safety-app literature helps explain why trip deviations may feel consequential to drivers, but it does little to explain why unfair ratings were the lowest-tolerance condition.
core_papers.key_finding: Personal safety apps are shifting from manual panic-button models to context-aware, sensor-fused, AI-assisted systems integrated with police or commercial monitoring centers, yet s

evidence_inventory.narrative: Grounded evidence inventory synthesized from actual corpus papers.
remaining_gaps: The survey's driver-side finding is intriguing but fragile. With only N=30 drivers, the rating-unfairness tolerance result can be used as a design signal or hypothesis, not a general behavioral conclusion. The algorithmic-management literature helps explain why this asymmetry matters, but it does not validate the specific magnitude or prevalence in a campus carpool context.

core_papers.key_finding: Games and gamification show promising potential to impact multiple climate change engagement dimensions simultaneously.

core_papers.title: Convergence of Gamification and Machine Learning: A Systematic Literature Review
core_papers.role: Contextualizing reference from category G: Convergence of Gamification and Machine Learning: A Systematic Literature Review
core_papers.key_finding: The convergence of machine learning and gamification is primarily applied in education, personalization, behavioral change, and context adaptation.

core_papers.title: Algorithmic Management in Organizations? From Edge Case to Center Stage
core_papers.role: Contextualizing reference from category J: Algorithmic Management in Organizations? From Edge Case to Center Stage
core_papers.key_finding: Algorithmic management creates a gray zone blurring employee-freelancer distinctions, allowing platforms to circumvent traditional employment regulations.

core_papers.title: Leveraging gamification technology to motivate environmentally responsible behavior: An empirical examination of Ant Forest
core_papers.role: Contextualizing reference from category G: Leveraging gamification technology to motivate environmentally responsible behav
core_papers.key_finding: Gamification promotes environmentally responsible behavior through psychological need satisfaction and belief/attitude persuasion in the postadoption stage.

evidence_inventory.narrative: Beat 6 is §5 CampusRide platform (artifact). The anchor is the pseudo-anchor local:CampusRideSystem2026. The spine references module names ('module:carpool', 'module:marketplace', etc.) and finding keys ('finding:F3', 'finding:F4', 'finding:F5'), not real paperIds. Narrative: (1) §5.1 platform overview with 6-module x 4-primitive matrix, (2) §5.2–5.7 each module gets one subsection at overview level, (3) §5.8 carpool deep-dive with four design decisions (identity, safety, rating fairness, gamification) each explicitly motivated by a survey finding. Use: we designed, we implemented, motivated by, in response to finding X. AVOID: effective, successful, proves, validates. Acknowledge: no deployment data; six-module claim acknowledges only carpool is deep-dived. (Primary-data / artifact beat: chain assembled from manual_core_inclusions.primary_anchors; contextualizing literature is listed as supporting rather than spine.)
remaining_gaps: The beat is sufficient to support that CampusRide was designed and implemented as an artifact, and that its carpool decisions are traceable to survey findings. It is not sufficient to support effectiveness, adoption, safety improvement, or sustainability claims, because no deployment evaluation is provided. The integrated multi-module framing is also more novel than corpus-backed.

evidence_inventory.title: Adversarial Scoping

core_papers.title: Algorithmic Management in Organizations? From Edge Case to Center Stage
core_papers.role: Opens the beat with the strongest general caution: once coordination is formalized into platform rules, the system may reproduce algorithmic-management harms and blur accountability rather than merely streamline matching.
core_papers.key_finding: Algorithmic management creates a gray zone blurring employee-freelancer distinctions, allowing platforms to circumvent traditional employment regulations.

core_papers.title: A Systematic Literature Review of Anti-Discrimination Design Strategies in the Digital Sharing Economy
core_papers.role: Provides the fairness heterogeneity bridge for the sample-skew paragraph: digital-platform outcomes vary across demographic groups because design choices can advantage some users over others.
core_papers.key_finding: DSE platforms embed design decisions that allow certain demographics to gain unfair advantage, identifiable through systematic synthesis of 58 interdisciplinary studies.

core_papers.title: Opaque Overwatch: How Food-Delivery Workers Make Sense of Algorithmic Management in the Gig Economy
core_papers.role: Anchors the no-deployment paragraph by showing that workers only learn, interpret, and resist algorithmic control in situated use, so non-deployed artifacts cannot support downstream claims about lived effects.
core_papers.key_finding: Technical opacity and power asymmetry in algorithmic management drive food-delivery gig workers to engage in sensemaking to understand and resist algorithmic control.

core_papers.title: Trust and power in Airbnb’s digital rating and reputation system
core_papers.role: Frames the .edu boundary as a scope limit, not a trust guarantee: platform reputation systems can embed power asymmetries and remain unreliable even when identity is partially known.
core_papers.key_finding: Digital reputation systems on platforms like Airbnb embed power asymmetries that undermine their reliability as fair communicators of trustworthy market behavior.

core_papers.title: Fulfillment of the Work Games: Warehouse Workers' Experiences with Algorithmic Management
core_papers.role: Closes the beat with the gamification warning: visible metrics and game-like tracking can invite adaptation, resistance, or strategic behavior, so cross-module points may induce gaming or fake trips.
core_papers.key_finding: Amazon fulfillment center workers develop nuanced resistance practices against labor-tracking algorithmic systems, extending CSCW understanding of algorithmic management beyond gig

evidence_inventory.narrative: Keep the tone explicitly concessive: use formulations such as 'may reproduce,' 'we acknowledge,' and 'scope-limited.' Do not convert any paragraph into a defense or mitigation claim. Paragraph 2 should directly name the sample skew (82% Mandarin-native; English-native students N=15) and admit that the cited evidence is indirect: it shows demographic heterogeneity in platform outcomes, not language-specific campus carpool behavior. Paragraph 3 must state that no real-world deployment compared the four design decisions, so no claims should be made about improved uptake, safety, fairness, or retention. Paragraph 4 should separate identity verification from competence, conduct, and governance. Paragraph 5 should say that points may invite gaming or fabricated activity; the support is adjacent platform evidence, which should be acknowledged as analogous rather than definitive.
remaining_gaps: This beat is well supported as a limitation section, though most of the external critique comes from labor, logistics, and public-transport domains rather than campus carpooling specifically. That is acceptable here because the point is not to prove failure, but to honestly delimit how formalization, ratings, incentives, and platform control can introduce new harms.
